title: Sales AE review
pros: Adobe is a great place to work if youâre in sales and want to sell strong products that actually deliver value. The brand opens doors, customers respect the platform, and the product roadmap (especially around AI) makes it easier to have strategic, executive-level conversations. The culture is genuinely supportive and collaborative â sales isnât a âlone wolfâ environment. Product, activation, CS, and marketing teams are smart, engaged, and willing to partner, which makes a big difference when working complex enterprise deals. Benefits and flexibility are also top-tier, and leadership generally trusts you to run your business.
cons: Like most large companies, things can move slowly and there are a lot of stakeholders involved, which can be frustrating when youâre trying to move fast on deals. The sales environment is evolving quickly, so priorities and coverage models can shift year to year. You need to be comfortable navigating change and advocating for yourself.

title: I don't get the hype
pros: Pay, Benefits, etc. All the things that they do to keep people around.
cons: Very siloed and difficult to get help or clarity on anything. Important emails go unanswered, crucial stakeholders required for meetings are frequently double booked, processes take weeks instead of days, and every time I try to get something done I encounter multiple unnecessary blockers, usually due to a breakdown of communication across teams. Enormous, bloated company, leaders don't want to make decisions so they just kick the can to another team. Too many different sites across timezones slows everything down. The back to office policy is ridiculous, unnecessary, and unevenly applied and we get no guidance during major storms or global events. And despite being a software company, our 3P software is dated and inefficient.

title: Software Engineer
pros: Strong brand reputation: Adobe is a well-established, respected tech company. Innovative product portfolio: Work on creative tools like Photoshop, Illustrator, and Adobe Sensei. Competitive compensation: Salaries, bonuses, and stock options are generally strong. Work-life balance: Many teams report reasonable hours and flexibility. Learning opportunities: Access to cutting-edge tech, internal mobility, and professional development.
cons: Large company bureaucracy: Slower decision-making and more red tape. Product legacy complexity: Older codebases can be hard to modernize. Team experience varies: Culture and workload can differ widely by org. Innovation pace: Some engineers feel constrained by product cycles or legacy systems.

title: Amazing benefits, downsides of a panicked corporaton
body: Amazing benefits, downsides of a panicked corporaton
pros: Love some of my co-workers. Benefits and time off generous.
cons: Hard to get promoted/not common. Minimal pay raises. Easy to get pigeon holed or stagnate in one job. Recently, a wave of anxiety about keeping up in the AI market has led to lots of panicked hiring, firing, and questionable/toxic behavior on the marketing side (top-down).

title: Adobe Layoffs May 2025
pros: Health, dental, and vision coverage are comprehensive on day one. Annual wellness reimbursement $600, professional development $2K and tuition reimbursement $10K. Generous parental leave, PTO, flexible work arrangements, and company-wide wellness days support a strong work-life balance. 401(k) matching is competitive, and employee stock purchase options.
cons: Adobe would benefit from stronger leadership accountability and clearer performance structures. Many teams lack defined OKRs, making it hard to align on priorities or measure success. A more transparent, outcomes-based goal-setting and review processâtied to actual business impactâwould help reduce favoritism and internal politics. It would also support fair, merit-based promotions and create a more equitable path for career advancement.

title: A Great Place to Work (with a few big-company trade-offs)
body: A Great Place to Work (with a few big-company trade-offs)
pros: Working at Adobe has been a genuinely rewarding experience for me. The company truly cares about its employees, not just in words, but in action. The benefits are fantastic, from comprehensive healthcare to generous PTO and wellness perks. During COVID, I really appreciated how the company prioritized stability and managed to avoid major layoffs. The people here are kind, collaborative, and always willing to help. And on a more everyday note, the food is amazing and subsidized, which makes coming into the office even better.
cons: That said, working at a company of Adobeâs size does come with its challenges. At times, things can move a bit slowly due to the number of stakeholders involved. It can also be hard to stand out in such a large and accomplished organization unless youâre very proactive about showcasing your work. Iâve learned that navigating the system and building internal visibility is key, but it does require extra effort. Overall, the experience is positive, but like any big company, it takes time to find your rhythm.

title: Don't underestimate your software engineers too much
body: Don't underestimate your software engineers too much
pros: A fair number of perks to the job. A fair amount of stability; WLB isn't too bad compared to other employers in the area
cons: Huge pay and annual incentive discrepancies between MLE vs your typical software engineer. This last review cycle really showed just how bad this is. many SE were given mediocre bumps and nearly zero refreshers over those with MLE. MLE makes 2-3x what a SE makes...something that is laughable today given that pretty much all ML work these days is tuning a prompt that gets fed into GPT. This is driving a lot of SE away from the company.

title: Adobeâs Still Solid, but Internal Culture and Competition Are Taking a Toll
body: Adobeâs Still Solid, but Internal Culture and Competition Are Taking a Toll
pros: â¢ Adobe remains a solid company overall, with strong and competent leadershipâShantanu and the C-suite executives are respected. â¢ Human creativity still matters, giving Adobe relevance despite rising competition. â¢ Management generally has deep institutional knowledge.
cons: â¢ Adobe has likely peaked in market dominance and is steadily losing ground to competitors offering similar or better tools for free, especially as AI evolves. â¢ As a mature, heavily matrixed organization, collaboration can be difficult, requiring you to strongly advocate for yourself and navigate significant red tape. â¢ Employee experience varies widely depending on team and manager, making the quality of your experience unpredictable. â¢ Lifecycle Marketing (LCM) org is notably toxic, with around 90% of individual contributors actively job hunting due to ongoing issues like subtle belittlement, micro-aggressions, and broken promises regarding career growth. â¢ Favoritism rather than merit tends to drive satisfaction among the few who are content in LCM.

title: Company in Decline
pros: Good work life balance. Generally easy low pressure environment which means you can slack off if you want, but the org suffers from too many low performing teams and individuals who have little consequences.
cons: It's accountants over engineers and technical excellence. The company ceased making great products and solely focuses on acquisitions and building a moat for existing tech. Customers are increasingly unhappy with quality and pricing. Since stock started falling, most efforts have been cost cutting. They cheap out everywhere now, including replacing all HR and payroll with chatbots. Get paid incorrectly? Go to a chatbot or wait over a week for a human response. Payroll issues happen more than should at a multi billion dollar company. Engineering is completely fractured into too many offshore teams that no one is then responsible for things actually working.

title: Don't join if you respect yourself
body: Don't join if you respect yourself
pros: You can get branded workwear just like at any fastfood or delivery service.
cons: At first you'll be in awe if you are an intern or never worked at a big company but that fades away quickly. You'll have to spend at least half of your time and resources to advocate for your importance. This is nonsense, but that's how it works at Adobe if you want to make a career, you don't actually need to work, just talk louder and it will be appreciated and rewarded with 7 figures. If you are a talented tech introvert, such people will ride you to the burnout and then replace you with fresh blood. At Adobe we: - ask employees for complete dedication and then throw them away - tell them it was a tough year and pocket their rewards - rig the bought surveys about being #1 place to work - respect slackers that talk a lot and disdain people that actually get the work done So if you are coming for experience you'd better go elsewhere. Adobe is not FAANG and not even close to that. This company has a stigma of tech laggard and it will be really hard to find a good job after it in tech domain.

title: Some good... a lot bad in my group
body: Some good... a lot bad in my group
pros: Good work/life balance, Pretty good benefits (Medical insurance is just OK), good commissions, Great PTO - they force employees to take time off, Well established tech brand. A lot of good people working there.
cons: - Poor management - sales managers don't support reps, are absent when needed and a lot of cross-brand managers control rep pricing. -Multiple Glassdoor reviews state "random layoffs occur annually..." this is true as it happened to me in less than 10 months at Adobe. - Sales is very process heavy and very "secretarial" as reps do clerical work (ie processing $50 orders) - Poor onboarding for sales roles and product training -MGT sends mixed messages and changes sales plays weekly -Internal processes and systems within some groups are surprisingly awful for a F500 company. Client Data is different in different systems.

pros: Youâll be at the intersection of data and community engagement, which makes the role dynamic and insightful. Adobeâs analytics team is top-notch, offering plenty of learning opportunities. title: Not a very good place to work
body: Not a very good place to work
pros: There is a lot of work, ideas and possibilities for professional growth
cons: Over the past five years, I have encountered several challenges that have impacted my job satisfaction and productivity. Salary and Work-Life Balance: The current compensation does not seem to align with the workload, and the work-life balance is often compromised. There have been instances where working beyond standard hours, including around the clock, has been expected. Management Experience: My experience with management has been particularly challenging. Iâve had two direct supervisors in the last five years, both of whom demonstrated poor leadership. They occasionally raised their voices, creating a stressful work environment. While my current manager is slightly better, it seems the company consistently hires managers who do not prioritize employee well-being. Strategic Planning: The companyâs approach to planning has been problematic, often leading to disorganization and inefficiencies.

title: There is a lot of toxicity from top down in the DX organization
body: There is a lot of toxicity from top down in the DX organization
pros: Great benefits, colleagues and peers can be great
cons: There are several male senior leaders who have been brought in who hold a very misogynistic approach to working with female leaders. Certain types of toxic behaviors - a Senior Leader who will only work with a male team member and dismisses working with any female member is not addressed. It is rewarded and this misogynistic and inequitable behavior creates a lot of opportunities for many male members of the team and pushes the females on the team into less visible roles which then comes to impact promotions, growth and recognition. Rather than dealing with the toxicity the behavior is allowed, normalized and in some ways, encouraged. Managers and Directors are often managing up and take walking orders from their superiors without offering much input or providing alternative ways to approach problems. They tactically do and the work is often not strategic but tactical and reactive. Adobe has an issue with ageism within certain parts of the organization. It is NOT a top place to work in my opinion.

title: Limited Advancement for Experienced (Older)/Minorities/Women at Adobe
body: Limited Advancement for Experienced (Older)/Minorities/Women at Adobe
pros: Great salary and benefits package
cons: * Limited Opportunities for Advancement: Promotions seem to favor a specific demographic regardless of actual performance. This creates a barrier for anyone above the age of 50, minorities and women seeking career growth. * Lack of Accountability: Some employees excel at managing upwards while contributing minimally. Their close relationships with management shield them from consequences. * Dismissive Culture: Openly addressing under performers can be misconstrued as negativity, while genuine concerns are sometimes disregarded. This can be frustrating for those who value a meritocratic work environment. * Innovation Stifled: Investment in cutting-edge solutions to expand Adobeâs product offerings seems to lack a strong follow-through on innovation. These acquired solutions often become outdated due to a lack of investment, hindering their potential.

title: Itâs fine

Adobe touts inclusive culture as âAdobe for allâ, but when I look around at the senior leadership teams, itâs a lot of older white and Indian men. There are pockets where diverse perspectives are valued, but the culture leans heavily to value male, straight, & white opinions. There is opportunity for improvement with increasing inclusivity and uplifting or amplifying under represented minority voices within leadership circles, on Senior Leadership, and E-teams. The onus should not be on the minority groups to make these changes. Better listening by execs of the Employee Network Leaders and HR leaders could help.

title: Fulfilling Experience
pros: As a Cybersecurity Specialist at Adobe, the work environment is both challenging and rewarding. Adobe prioritizes security in its products and internal systems, offering a unique opportunity to work on a variety of projects that have a tangible impact on the company and its customers. The emphasis on innovation allows for the exploration of new security technologies and practices, making every day a learning opportunity. Adobe supports professional development, offering access to training resources and conferences that are pivotal for staying ahead in the cybersecurity field. The team culture is collaborative and supportive, with a strong emphasis on work-life balance, which is highly valued in the high-stress domain of cybersecurity.
cons: While the opportunities for growth and learning are significant, the fast-paced nature of the work can sometimes lead to periods of high stress, especially during product launches or when responding to security incidents. The expectation to stay ahead of rapidly evolving cyber threats requires a continuous and sometimes exhausting effort to learn and adapt. Additionally, as with many large organizations, navigating the corporate structure and bureaucracy can occasionally slow down decision-making processes or lead to challenges in implementing changes swiftly.

title: Work Experience
pros: Working at Adobe as a People Analyst was an enriching experience that allowed me to delve deep into the heart of the company's culture. From day one, I was immersed in a dynamic environment where collaboration and innovation were at the forefront. As a People Analyst, I had the privilege of not only analyzing data but also deciphering the intricate patterns of human behavior within the organization. The culture at Adobe fosters growth and development, encouraging employees to think outside the box and push boundaries.
cons: One significant aspect was the fast-paced nature of the tech industry, which often translated into tight deadlines and high-pressure situations. Balancing the demands of analyzing data while meeting project timelines could sometimes be overwhelming. Additionally, like any large corporation, bureaucratic processes could slow down decision-making and implementation of initiatives. Despite efforts to foster a culture of inclusivity, there were still instances where hierarchical structures hindered open communication and collaboration.

title: for the money and to use as a step up
body: for the money and to use as a step up
pros: Salary is not insufficient; it is a well-known company; for now, you can use Adobe software for free. However, it's better not to use it for personal and private usage.
cons: Everything will be in your manager's hands, so depending on your manager, your life can be good or hell. It's a big company and you're just another number and they will make you fill a form answering that they are the best or else you will be in HR black list. They will constantly put you on a PIP (performance improvement plan); if you don't archive, you will feel they put you on a kick-out list. HR is non-existent, and everything negative you say about anything will be the company's problem.

title: Do not recommend if you are a religious person.
body: Do not recommend if you are a religious person.
pros: Vision and health insurance was good.
cons: During COVID I was asked to fill out a long questionnaire detailing my religious reasons for not wanting the vaccine. I submittted the form, and it was never addressed. Instead, I was not allowed in corporate offices or to travel during most of COVID. I feel this was discriminatory. Adobe talks about "Adobe for all" in internal communications and how they welcome and celebrate everyone. I did not feel welcomed or celebrated. I think this is a double standard and typical corporate speak. I do not recommend them as an employer if you care about personal religious freedom.

title: Maximum job security, minimum opportunities for career growth
body: Maximum job security, minimum opportunities for career growth
pros: - It's really hard to get PIPed/fired - Everyone's generally really nice and I've never really encountered workplace toxicity that wasn't immediately addressed - Work life balance is about as good as it gets - Despite commands from leadership, hardly anyone is working on-site >50%
cons: - No one ever quits or gets fired, so there's little opportunity for upward or lateral mobility - The larger/older the codebase, the more difficult it is to contribute, and the more fragile the automation/build pipeline - The fact that so many still WFH makes it really difficult to get things from people when you need them -- expect Slack responses in either 2 minutes or 2 days

title: Beware of Sales at Adobe
pros: USED to be a great company. Growth goals are unrealistic with current products.
cons: Great company a few years ago but Sales is struggling to meet unrealistic sales goals. Great people are being recruited away from other companies to join only to find the accounts they have been given are non-producing, the sales goals are unrealistic and the money is just not there like it used to be. DON'T leave a good job to join this circus of leadership, only to be let go for not meeting their inflated performance goals.

title: Bait and switch -
pros: Adobe is the most beautiful fools gold I have ever experienced. They poached me and many others I know from stable positions at other companies. Threw money at us to be on a team that was constant chaos and dysfunction. Only to lay off everyone SIX MONTHS later. Run. Donât walk away from this nightmare. Unless you are a good ole boy fresh out of college. They love them. Salary was amazing but to be without a job wasnât worth leaving a stable mid-range level position at another company.
cons: Everything. When you think of Adobe, you think of photoshop and illustrator as well as tech and stability. They donât want us talking about it, but they are internally very cishet WHITE Mormon male focused. If you do not fit this or agree with their values, you WILL be removed. I have seen it time and time again. I have heard that the managers are secretly pretty discriminatory towards people of color and lgbt. The people of color in management positions are few and far between. If there is one, like one of the directors in mid-market, they are tap dancing for said cishet folks above them. Not progressive as they make it seem.

title: Great Place to Work if You Land on The Right Clique
body: Great Place to Work if You Land on The Right Clique
pros: - Incredibly talented and friendly coworkers - Great mentors all over the place - Team is agile and gets the job done - Clear vision and prioritization from leadership - You get to work on products used all over the world. You will make an impact. - Great pay and benefits - Hybrid/remote work culture - Lots of days off: unlimited PTO (works if you get a chill manager like mine), wellness day off every other month, company wide week off (5 days) in summer and another one in winter, sabbaticals every 5 years - There's a reason why Adobe is known for retaining employees for 10, 15, 25 years. Adobe cares about work/life balance and employees mental and physical health.

title: Great company, great co-workers, great position.
body: Great company, great co-workers, great position.
pros: Adobe is one of the best employee-focused companies that I have ever worked for. The benefits package is more than reasonable. My co-workers are great people that really want to help our customers. The company very open to at least part-time remote work. The culture is great and the fringe benefits are worth it.
cons: Management is very focused on the bottom line, and does not hesitate to adjust and modify a position if it is not meeting expectations. CEO would like everyone to return to work in an Adobe office for at least part of the work-week.
role: Current employee - Senior Technical Account Manager

title: Amazing company to work for!
pros: Amazing corporate culture (happy colleagues), great competitive and high-end innovative products, sharp long-term vision. Hardly seen any layoffs even on hard times like early 2023! Benefits are amazing for FTEs: very generous PTO, yearly $10k Education Fund for Long-term learning experiences, $1K Prof Development Fund for short-term learning experiences, $600 Wellness Benefits (Health and Equipment), ESPP with 15% discount. If you are a high performer, you might earn RSUs.
cons: Constant issues with commissions pay - all fixable/paid accordingly, but definitely time consuming to troubleshooting. Regular pay (salary) could be a little higher comparing to market - I wouldn't say it's bad, but definitely average comparting to big tech.

title: Depends on your group, like any large company
body: Depends on your group, like any large company
pros: I work in the Creative Cloud side of Adobe and from an impact on the industry perspective, Adobe leads the way as far as places you want to be - the reach and impact from Photoshop to the video tools is amazing and it's incredible to be part of.
cons: As Adobe grows, the large company politics gets worse and worse. I am considering leaving because of the amount of politics I see. I come from a small company background where this doesn't happen (other challenges for sure) - it is extremely frustrating.

title: Great benefits and WLB

title: Great Corporate Culture - Difficult Project Culture
body: Great Corporate Culture - Difficult Project Culture
pros: Company has really good benefits. Maternity and Paternity leave is the longest I've ever seen. Has employee stock purchase program that is really awesome. Office is really fun and has a lot of perks including a great cafe and places to take a break. Provides educational, professional, and health reimbursement programs.
cons: It's a very large company which can make it difficult to complete projects. Everything is very connected so it's hard to get buy-off from impacted teams and resources (budget, employees, etc.) are low.

title: A lot of room for improvement
body: A lot of room for improvement
pros: -Great company culture -Great benefits -Flexibility -Remote work -Much of job responsibility negotiated with manager
cons: -EAs are "linked" to their manager so limited promotions and salary increases -Only Exec Team EAs are granted the "Sr EA" title, despite years of service and experience - EA role treated as "only" a steppingstone to another career path, e.g. Program Manager and not a career in itself -No developed career path, barely distinguishable job levels, no understanding of how to be promoted -No ability to be promoted -EAs are treated like a 1950s secretarial pool where they are assigned Sr Dir and VPs instead of having a discussion about current workload, career path, etc. -Managers do not understand their EAs abilities, or possible job responsibilities -Managers do not understand that admins are eligible for bonuses, RSUs, etc.

title: It's real - Adobe is a great place and good company to work for
body: It's real - Adobe is a great place and good company to work for
pros: Adobe has made great efforts around inclusion and diversity not just in it's talent pool /hiring practices , also thru grassroots mobility. Utilizing their employee networks to spread the word, provide input and feedback as well as encouraging people to learn about biases and how to practice mitigation of them . Benefits are great , especially the sabbatical eligibility every 5 years of continuous service Contracted employees while not getting access to all what is available to a full time employee , overall they are treated well based on my observations and conversations with current and past Adobe contractors
cons: Like other industries and companies there are bad actors everywhere . Good news is those types of individuals generally do not last long . We have the same or similar issues/challenges most tech companies and companies in general have - our advantage is really great people are here , who are motivated to take on and/or solve for those issues
role: Current employee - Senior Business Solution Analyst

title: Great company!
pros: Solid company financials and outlook. Very stable place to work with better than average comp. Great place to get a leg up in the creative and/or enterprise software space. Youâll have the benefit of a class leading, name brand company on your resume that will help in your next career move. Many teams are also very transparent and open to helping you grow in your career even if it means helping to transfer to a new team you are interested in after youâve had some tenure with them. Last thing to mention is exceptional work life balance. Most teams purely care about output and offer flexibility on when and where you work.
cons: Lots of politics. Many employees pour their heart and soul into their work which is great but can lead to sensitivity from some teams or individuals when being critical or offering feedback. This is not a major con but just something to be aware of. The good thing is that most people are very receptive and understand criticism and critique is with good intention.
